title: Chapter 5 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Methods, Parameters, Arguments, and Retun Values!

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a method in programming?
options: To make the code run slower so humans can read it.
To bundle reusable chunks of code so you don't repeat yourself.
questions.explanation: Methods follow the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) principle by allowing you to write code once and run it anywhere.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to DEFINE a new method in Ruby?
questions.explanation: The `def` keyword stands for 'define'.

questions.question: Once you define a method (e.g., `def greet ... end`), what happens if you never "call" it?
options: It runs automatically when the program finishes.
It runs exactly once at the top of the script.
Nothing happens. The code inside is entirely ignored by Ruby.
questions.explanation: Defining a method just teaches Ruby the command. You must explicitly 'call' the method name to execute its code.

questions.question: What does DRY stand for in programming?
questions.explanation: DRY stands for Don't Repeat Yourself, advocating for reusable code architectures like methods.

questions.question: What is the purpose of method parameters?
options: To control the speed of the method.
To act as temporary slots/variables waiting to receive outside data.
To return values to the main program.
To track how many times the method has been called.
questions.explanation: Parameters are defined next to the method name to catch incoming data (arguments).

questions.question: In Ruby, you MUST provide an argument for every single parameter a method asks for, unless it has a default value.
questions.explanation: If a method expects 2 arguments and you only give it 1, Ruby will throw an ArgumentError and crash.

questions.question: How do you define a 'Default Parameter'?
questions.explanation: You assign a default value right inside the parameter parentheses using `=`.

questions.question: If you define `def order(type = "Coffee")`, but the user calls `order("Tea")`, what happens?
options: It throws an error because a default is already set.
questions.explanation: A default value is only used if NO argument is passed. The user's input ("Tea") always overrides the default.

questions.question: Which keyword officially spits data back OUT of a method so the rest of the program can use that data?
questions.explanation: The `return` keyword hands data backward to the place where the method was called.

questions.question: What happens to code written INSIDE a method but directly BELOW a `return` keyword?
options: It becomes "dead code" and is never executed because `return` terminates the method.
It is saved for the next time the method is called.
questions.explanation: As soon as Ruby hits `return`, the method ends instantly. Anything below it is ignored entirely.

questions.question: If you don't explicitly write the word `return`, a Ruby method will return nothing at all.
questions.explanation: Ruby uses Implicit Return. It ALWAYS automatically returns the result of the very last line of code evaluated.

questions.question: What feature allows Ruby methods to hand back data without typing the exact word `return`?
questions.explanation: Implicit Return means Ruby automatically assumes the last line of your block is what you want handed back.

questions.question: To get multiple pieces of data into a method, you separate the parameters using a _____.
questions.explanation: Multiple parameters are separated by commas `(param1, param2, param3)`.

questions.question: What does the concept of "Variable Scope" refer to regarding methods?
options: How much memory a variable takes up.
The fact that a variable created inside a method cannot be seen or used outside of that method.
The length of the variable's name.
Whether a variable is an Integer or a String.
questions.explanation: Methods act as fortresses. They protect their internal variables from the outside world, and can't see the outside world themselves.

questions.question: If you create a variable `total = 50` inside your main script, and then call a method `def show_total`, can that method automatically see `total = 50`?
options: Only if the variable is an Integer.
No, the method cannot see the outside variable unless you pass it in as an argument.
Yes, but it takes double memory.
questions.explanation: Because of variable scope, methods are blind to outside code. You must push the data into them intentionally via arguments.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between `puts` and `return`?
options: They do exactly the same thing.
`puts` is for strings, `return` is for integers.
`puts` only flashes text on the screen for a human to read. `return` hands actual usable data back to the computer program.
`return` prints to the screen in bold text.
questions.explanation: A very common beginner trap! `puts` is purely visual display. If you want to use the method's result in further math or logic, you MUST use `return`.

questions.question: If a method is defined with both mandatory variables and optional (default) variables, what order should they be listed in?
options: Mandatory ones first, Defaults at the end.
Defaults first, Mandatory ones at the end.
questions.explanation: Convention dictates that `def example(mandatory1, mandatory2, optional=1)` is the cleanest way.

questions.question: Is `def MyMethod` considered good styling in Ruby?
questions.explanation: Ruby strongly prefers `snake_case`, so it should be `def my_method`.

questions.question: What is the difference between an Argument and a Parameter?
options: An argument is the real data you pass in. A parameter is the placeholder variable catching that data.
A parameter is the real data. An argument is the placeholder.
Arguments are only used in loops.
questions.explanation: You define a method with Parameters (the empty buckets). You call a method wielding Arguments (the water filling the buckets).

questions.question: Every method definition must be sealed cleanly by using the _____ keyword at the bottom.
questions.explanation: Similar to loops and `if` statements, methods require an `end` to define their block.
